title: Stars of Akarios - A casual unboxing
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=enR1CfAS5zo

Daniel (@dungeondive) unboxes Stars of Akarios, a big narrative campaign game he backed three years earlier — and he's candid that his patience for sprawling story epics has since evaporated, so he half-expects to sell it after a fair try. The 'Gloomhaven in space' shorthand shows on the surface: stacks of combat modifier decks he's lukewarm on, calling many of the cards dry and mechanical, space he'd rather have seen spent on story and events. But there's plenty he likes on sight: standees (offered as an alternative to minis, which delights him), well-organised dual-layer player boards, and a refreshingly small pile of chits and tiles for a game this size. What genuinely sparks his imagination are the exploration systems — Seventh Continent-style planetary exploration cards across nine environments, and larger star-system maps for space travel — which he says are the real reason he backed. He notes the slim 35-page rulebook, the Gloomhaven-sized scenario book broken into three acts with a learn-as-you-go tutorial and QR-code videos, and a small hex map board that might actually fit his notoriously small table. A cautiously optimistic first look.

----

Daniel would rather designers spend the component budget on story and events than on big decks of combat modifier cards. Where should a campaign game's production money go first for you?
